title: Dalea minutifolia
intro: Dalea minutifolia, also known as Prickly Milkpea, is a perennial shrub native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, belonging to the legume family (Fabaceae). This plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including inflammation and fever, due to its anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties. The bioactive constituents of Dalea minutifolia include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, such as quercetin and ursolic acid, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, Dalea minutifolia is commonly prepared as a tea or tincture, and its extracts have also been used in traditional remedies, such as infusions and salves, to treat wounds and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Dalea minutifolia is classified as a species within the genus Dalea, which belongs to the family Fabaceae. This plant is commonly known as a type of prairie rose or a shrub. The genus Dalea is further classified within the subfamily Faboideae, and the family Fabaceae is a large and diverse group of flowering plants known as legumes. The classification of Dalea minutifolia reflects its evolutionary relationships to other plants in the legume family. Understanding the classification of a plant like Dalea minutifolia helps scientists to study its characteristics and behavior in a broader context.

In some cultures, Dalea minutifolia is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may contribute to its therapeutic uses. However, its medicinal properties and uses require further scientific investigation to be fully understood.
